# Ten Video Seed Ideas for the Channel

*Synthesized overnight. Generated by:*
1. *Three research-survey agents (public NBA data sources; computer-vision
   feasibility; analytical-YouTube whitespace).*
2. *A v0 draft of 10 ideas.*
3. *Five viewer-persona pressure-tests on the v0 (quant Discord reader,
   sharp sports bettor, casual NBA fan, ex-front-office analyst, YouTube
   creator strategist).*
4. *Five deep-research agents on the strongest survivors + two replacement
   candidates.*

*The final ten below are ordered to mix tiers — strongest singletons up
front, with variety in difficulty, audience, and aesthetic throughout.
Each idea has: hook, channel fit, persona scoring, headline-number
candidates, data sources, methodology sketch, risks, prior work to
differentiate against, and effort estimate.*

*Drafts and pressure-tests are preserved at `IDEAS_v0.md` and in the
agent transcripts under `/tmp/claude-1000/.../tasks/`.*

---

## Quick Reference

| # | Title | Tier | Effort | Strongest persona | Weakest |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **The Apron Game Tree** | S | 6w | All five | — |
| 2 | **Two Stars, Two Halves** (Maymin update) | S | 4w | Bettor / FO | — |
| 3 | **Tanking Doesn't Build Champions. It Builds Trade Chips.** | A+ | 4w | Strategist / FO | — |
| 4 | **EPV From Scratch** (Lague-mode flagship) | A+ | 8–10w | Quant / Strategist | Casual |
| 5 | **The Coach Effect** | A | 3w | Quant / FO | — |
| 6 | **The NBA Pulled Its Tracking Data. Here's What's Still On GitHub.** | A | 6w | Casual / Strategist | Bettor |
| 7 | **The Two Load-Management Studies That Can't Both Be Right** | A | 3w | Quant / Strategist | Bettor |
| 8 | **Whose Defense Is Real? The Matchup-Data Audit** | A− | 3w | Quant / FO | Casual |
| 9 | **Wasted Gravity** (the half the NBA's stat misses) | B+ | 5w | Casual / FO | Bettor |
| 10 | **The Ring-Chase Tax** (reimbursement-adjusted) | B+ | 3w | Casual / Strategist | — |

*Tier S = greenlight tomorrow. Tier A = needs the sharpening noted in
"risks." Tier B = solid but ceiling-capped.*

*Effort = weeks of solo work, including animation. Calibrate from your
own NPV-project velocity.*

---

# IDEA 1 — The Apron Game Tree

> "I ran ten thousand simulations of what happens when an NBA contender
> crosses the second apron. The median contending window is 2.3 years
> before a forced star trade."

**Channel fit:** Direct continuation of the contracts work. Primer-style
simulation, Sebastian Lague-style "watch me build the simulator,"
Thinking-Basketball-style methodology defense. Visually amazing in Manim.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant (top 3):** "Strongest visual-bait on the list. Publish the
  code, sensitivity-test the aging-curve choice."
- **Bettor (top 3):** "Futures alpha. Forced-breakup probabilities
  re-price 2-3 year championship odds the market sleeps on."
- **Casual:** "10K simulations sounds cool but 'apron game tree'
  sounds like a tax form. Pick one team, name it in the title."
- **FO insider (TOP 1):** "Every FO is running this simulation right
  now with worse tools. I'd send this to my GM."
- **Strategist (top 3):** "Highest visual ceiling of any idea. CBA
  discourse is durable through 2027."

**Headline candidates (in increasing risk):**
1. **"Median contending window for a roster crossing the 2nd apron is
   2.3 years before a forced star trade."** (P(top-3-cap-hit player
   moved by year 3) ≥ 75% in the sim.)
2. **"The frozen-pick rule alone destroys ~$18M of expected asset
   value per season above the 2nd apron."** (Compare unprotected 7-out
   pick value to frozen-then-30th conversion.)
3. **"Only 1 in 12 second-apron rosters wins a title before being
   forced to break up."** (P(championship | apron-locked) vs.
   P(championship | first-apron-with-flexibility).)

**Data sources:**
- Spotrac per-team multi-year contracts (already partially scraped).
- APBRmetrics + DARKO/EPM aging priors.
- Historical star-trade record 2014–2025 (hand-compile, ~50 cases).
- NBA cap projections (Bobby Marks publishes annually).

**Methodology sketch:**
- Build a roster-evolution simulator with stochastic aging,
  injury priors, and trade-frequency conditional on cap state.
- Enforce a checklist of CBA mechanics:
  2nd apron threshold, frozen 7-out 1st-round pick, salary-aggregation
  ban, 100% trade matching above 1st apron, TMLE hard-cap, NTMLE/BAE/S&T
  hard-cap-at-1st-apron, repeater tax cliff, 140%-vs-175% extension/FA
  raise gap, vet extension eligibility windows, Designated Veteran
  ("supermax") eligibility, trade kickers, Over-38 rule, Beal-stretch
  precedent. (Full list with CBA section references in the deep-research
  doc.)
- Anchor narrative on three case studies: Suns
  (closed-loop cautionary tale), Celtics (mid-collapse, post-Tatum
  Achilles), Nuggets (warning shot, Jokić extension pending).

**Risks:**
- Trade frequency conditional on the second apron is unknowable — the
  apron has existed for ~2 seasons; n ≈ 4 team-seasons of data.
- CBA mechanics are brutally specific. Get one wrong and cap-Twitter
  ratios you. Mitigate by publishing the code.

**Prior work to differentiate against:** Yossi Gozlan (Third Apron —
hand-built mock offseasons, no Monte Carlo), Bobby Marks (ESPN — single-
team written outlooks, no simulator), Spotrac (static apron tracker).
**No public Monte Carlo apron simulator exists.** This is a real gap.

**Effort:** ~6 weeks (1 week data, 2 weeks simulator, 1 week sensitivity
analysis, 2 weeks animation). Publish the code.

---

# IDEA 2 — Two Stars, Two Halves (the Maymin Update)

> "When teams add a second offensive star, the duo retains about 70% of
> additive value. When teams add a second defensive star, they retain
> 115%. The math has been sitting in a 2013 paper unvisualized for 12
> years."

**Channel fit:** Builds on the existing contracts methodology, lands a
specific headline number (the Maymin coefficients exist, you just have
to replicate them on modern data), and answers a question every fan has.
Discord-postable.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant (with reframe):** "Fine if you cite Witus 2008 on screen.
  Frame as 17-year-old finding re-estimated with modern lineup data +
  skill-overlap decomposition."
- **Bettor (TOP 1):** "Most actionable idea on the doc. Fade win totals
  on newly-built two-ball-handler teams. This fires every trade
  deadline."
- **Casual:** "Use famous duos — Harden+Westbrook, KD+Kyrie, Giannis+Lopez.
  Make me feel something."
- **FO insider (top 3):** "Defensive duos super-add only when skills
  complement — switch + playmaker, rim + perimeter. Two rim protectors
  diminish like rebounding. Split offense and defense — that's the
  headline."
- **Strategist:** "Counterintuitive number in the title = catnip.
  Risk: methodology may not survive Discord without Bayesian shrinkage
  shown on screen."

**Headline candidates:**
1. **"Offensive star duos retain ~70% of their additive value.
   Defensive duos retain ~115%."** (Direct Maymin extrapolation.)
2. **"Adding a second high-usage ball-handler costs ~1.25 pts/100 per
   shared usage point — about 0.8 wins of his solo value."** (Witus
   2008; bulletproof.)
3. **"Switchable / playmaking defensive duos add 30% more than the sum
   of their parts. Two rim protectors don't."** (Maps to Maymin's
   +0.307 defensive ball-handling synergy.)

**Data sources:**
- `nba_api.LeagueDashLineups` with `group_quantity=2`, multi-season
  pool (2019–2025), 500-possession minimum.
- DARKO + EPM individual ratings as the additive baseline.
- Snarr-style Bayesian RAPM as shrinkage prior.

**Methodology sketch:**
- For every two-man "star pair" since 2019 (both players top-30
  offensive or defensive RAPM), compute observed lineup ORtg/DRtg
  vs. an additive-RAPM baseline.
- Ridge-regularize with L-RAPM's λ_off=4000 / λ_def=6000 (defense needs
  more shrinkage; lineup defense stabilizes slower).
- Decompose by skill overlap (two ball-handlers, two rim-protectors,
  switch + playmaker, etc.).
- Anchor on: Harden+Westbrook (predicted sub-add, confirmed), KD+Steph
  (off-ball KD, super-add confirmed), Giannis+Lopez (rim+drop,
  super-add confirmed), Wemby+AD 2026 (two rim anchors, predicted to
  diminish, will be tested).

**Risks:**
- Lineup-RAPM with n < 500 possessions for pair-specific lineups is noise.
- Bayesian prior choice will drive the headline number — sensitivity
  analysis is mandatory.

**Prior work to differentiate:** Eli Witus 2008 (Count the Basket —
rebounding and usage diminishing returns); Ben Falk's Stabilized Net
Rating framework at CTG; Seth Partnow chapter in *The Midrange Theory*;
Maymin Maymin Shen 2013 "NBA Chemistry" (has the coefficients but is
12 years old and was never animated). **Cite Witus on screen.** Frame
as: "the founding finding, re-estimated with modern data, split into
offense and defense for the first time on video."

**Effort:** ~4 weeks. (1 week data, 1 week regression + shrinkage,
1 week duo case studies, 1 week animation.)

---

# IDEA 3 — Tanking Doesn't Build Champions. It Builds Trade Chips.

> "Of the last 10 completed tank cycles, [N] rostered a top-15 player
> within 5 years. All but one came from drafted picks. Zero came from
> trading the stockpile for an established star."

**Channel fit:** Strong-opinion title (Jxmy register), elegant arithmetic
(Sebastian Lague register), Discord-shareable thesis, casual-fan
accessible.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant ("dead" → "alive with reframe"):** "The original 'tanking
  doesn't work' framing is the most done idea on YouTube. The asset-
  accumulation reframe is the one fresh angle nobody owns."
- **Bettor:** "Futures play. Build a 'post-tank franchise' tracker,
  fade the win total in the year the market starts buying the rebuild
  (think 2024 Spurs, 2025 Pistons)."
- **Casual (top 3):** "Tanking is a scam, the data proves it. Show me
  the Process Sixers, the Thunder rebuild, the Pistons. I want the
  receipts."
- **FO insider (top 3):** "Inside a building, tanking is a 3-year
  asset-accumulation bet, not a 7-year championship bet. This reframe
  is exactly how owners ask the question."
- **Strategist (TOP 1):** "Highest expected views ceiling on the doc.
  Topic does the heavy lifting. Risk: title must match what the data
  actually says."

**Headline candidates:**
1. **"Of the last 10 completed tank cycles, [N] rostered a top-15
   player within 5 years. All but one came from drafted picks — zero
   came from trading the stockpile."** (Requires you to audit the
   cohort by hand; ~12 cases, tractable.)
2. **"The Stepien Rule caps a star-trade package at ~4 firsts. The
   current market price for a fringe All-NBA player is ~4 firsts.
   Every pick beyond your fifth has near-zero marginal trade value.
   The Process peaked at 9 firsts/swaps — they converted less than
   half."** (Pure arithmetic + audit.)
3. **"Post-2019 lottery reform, 0 of 7 worst-record teams secured the
   #1 pick. Expected value of being the worst has fallen 44%. Tanking
   volume is at an all-time high."** (Sportico-adjacent but reframed.)

**Data sources:**
- Historical standings + playoff records (Basketball-Reference).
- Draft pick movement (RealGM trade database, Pelton draft pick value
  chart).
- Star-trade record + asset bundle composition (hand-compile from
  press archives).

**Methodology sketch:**
- Define a "tank cycle": team with ≥ 90% probability of missing playoffs
  by Jan 15 AND bottom-5 win%, for 2+ consecutive seasons.
- For each cycle, enumerate the asset bundle at peak (picks owned + swap
  rights + Stepien-constrained tradeability).
- Track 5-year outcome: did a top-15 EPM/RAPTOR player land on the
  roster via (a) drafted pick, or (b) trade with ≥ 40% of outgoing value
  from tank-era assets.
- Cross-tabulate conversion rate by route.
- Anchor case studies: OKC (drafted SGA/J-Dub/Holmgren, didn't even
  need to use the bundle), Sixers Process (asset-rich, conversion-poor),
  Pistons (in-progress, pure-draft success), Spurs (too early).

**Risks:**
- Endogeneity — bad teams tank *because* they're bad. Address with
  matched-counterfactual or be honest that the comparison is
  observational.
- Small sample (n ≈ 12). Lean into named cases rather than aggregate
  significance claims.

**Prior work:** Ben Taylor's tanking video; Owen Phillips at The F5;
Kevin Pelton's draft pick trade-value chart (2015/2017/2024);
Sportico April 2026 piece; Wharton tanking papers (Sherman 2018,
Bewtra/Brill 2024). **All ask "did tanking produce a Finals team?"
None compute the asset-conversion rate.** Frame your video as
the FO-internal question made public.

**Effort:** ~4 weeks. Most time goes to hand-compiling the
asset-bundle/conversion ledger.

---

# IDEA 4 — EPV From Scratch (the Lague Flagship)

> "In 2014, four researchers at Sloan figured out how to put a real-time
> price on every NBA possession. Then the NBA bought the data feed and
> killed the public model. I rebuilt EPV from scratch on the last public
> tracking data — and learned why nobody uses it now."

**Channel fit:** This is the channel-defining video. Compounds the brand.
The kind of piece new subscribers binge after they find you.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant (TOP 1):** "Biggest moat, hardest to copy, methodology
  flagship. Drop the 2025-26 leaderboard claim and keep it as a
  methodology + viz piece on a specific era."
- **Bettor:** "No betting hook from 2015 data. Methodology piece —
  I'd watch but wouldn't bet."
- **Casual ("dead"):** "8-10 weeks of micro/macro model is the whole
  video for me. EPV means nothing. Reframe around the payoff: 'why
  your favorite player never gets the ball in the 4th.'"
- **FO insider:** "Teams stopped caring about EPV-the-paper around 2018
  because Second Spectrum's productized version replaced it. The
  'leaky possession' framing is good — push there."
- **Strategist (top 3):** "Slower burn, won't crush week one, will
  compound for 18 months as the channel's calling card. Lague's Coding
  Adventures format applies directly."

**Headline candidates:**
1. **"EPV was a 2014 idea built for a 2014 NBA. Rebuilding it taught
   me why nobody uses it now."** (Honest post-mortem framing — most
   robust to the data-staleness critique.)
2. **"The 14-state shortcut: how Cervone made 25Hz tracking tractable."**
   (Methodology-flagship framing — landing the coarsening trick.)
3. **"Every NBA possession's value moves like a stock ticker. Here are
   the five biggest dropped-EPV decisions of the 2015 playoffs."**
   (Specific old-data findings as the payoff.)

**Data sources:**
- HuggingFace `dcayton/nba_tracking_data_15_16` (~600 games, 25Hz,
  JSON, PBP-merged with documented sync bleed). Cleaner starting point.
- `neilmj/BasketballData` raw 7z dumps through Jan 2016. Backup
  reference.
- `dcervone/EPVDemo` — Cervone's own R/INLA demo. **Inference-only**;
  no training pipeline. Use as architecture reference.
- `linouk23/NBA-Player-Movements`, `dblackrun/pbpstats` — supporting
  parsing infrastructure.

**Methodology sketch (8-week full, with 4-week checkpoint):**
- W1: data pipeline (JSON parse, PBP sync, possession segmentation,
  coordinate normalization).
- W2: 14-state coarsened TPM from PBP outcomes (the trick that makes
  the math tractable).
- W3: NMF spatial bases + player-similarity matrix H.
- W4: **Checkpoint — simplified XGBoost frame-instant EPV.** If
  full hierarchical hazard model is overrunning, this version still
  ships a 90%-of-the-wow-factor video. Drop microtransitions, treat
  EPV as supervised regression on 25Hz state.
- W5–6: macro hazard models (shot/pass/TO) via INLA in R or PyMC.
- W7: micro-transition stochastic process.
- W8: Monte Carlo inference loop + validation.
- W9–10: Manim animation + writing.

**Lague-style failure beats (lean into these on camera):**
- JSON sync bleed (tracking starts before PBP events; off-by-one).
- Possession segmentation misfires (offensive rebounds, jump balls).
- The stateless Markov toy gives Curry the same value standing in the
  corner as pulling up. Motivates the full model.
- INLA scaling cliff past N games. Forces the H-matrix pooling
  explanation.
- MC variance jitter at 50 draws. Tune to 200.
- The defensive model is embarrassingly thin. The honest "I gave up
  here" beat.

**Risks:**
- Stale data. The video can't credibly claim "Player X is the league's
  worst EPV decision-maker today" using 2015 data. Either workaround
  with a 2024-25 box-score-derived decision proxy, or commit to the
  methodology-post-mortem framing.
- 8+ weeks with no checkpoint = high risk of shipping nothing.
  Hard-commit to the W4 simplified version as a fallback.

**Prior work:** Cervone, D'Amour, Bornn, Goldsberry 2014 (Sloan,
JASA 1408.0777); Howard Baek's animated explainer; Fernandez et al.
2019 soccer DL-EPV. **No end-to-end public EPV training pipeline
exists.** The Cervone demo deliberately ships precomputed
components. This is genuinely greenfield work.

**Effort:** 8–10 weeks. Highest-effort idea on the list. The
identity-piece tradeoff.

---

# IDEA 5 — The Coach Effect

> "A 2025 peer-reviewed paper computed coach fixed-effects controlling
> for roster talent. The spread between best and worst coach is 46
> wins — bigger than the player MVP-to-replacement gap. Nick Nurse adds
> more wins than peak LeBron's Win Share. Nobody has shown this on
> video."

**Channel fit:** Replicable academic finding, never animated, lands
specific named coaches with surprising effect sizes. Pairs with #8
(Matchup Difficulty) as "the things the public underrates" series.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant:** Replacement candidate, not in v0 — new based on JQAS 2025
  paper. Replicable methodology (BART on ΔtVORP), public data.
- **Bettor:** "Coaching-change futures are a real market. Quantified
  coach impact gives you a baseline for fading 'new coach bounce'
  hype."
- **Casual:** "Frank Vogel underrated, Pop top decile — those are
  arguments I'll have on the timeline."
- **FO insider:** "Internal teams have priors here, but nobody publishes.
  This would actually circulate inside buildings."
- **Strategist:** "Named coaches in thumbnails. Strong Twitter share
  potential. Will get framed as the 'Nick Nurse +20 wins' video."

**Headline candidates:**
1. **"Nick Nurse adds 20 wins above a replacement coach — more than
   peak LeBron's Win Share."** (Strong, small-sample-flagged.)
2. **"Frank Vogel is 89th percentile coaching, 75th percentile winning.
   The roster is hiding him."** (The contrarian individual case.)
3. **"The spread between best and worst NBA coach is 46 wins. That's
   bigger than the gap between MVP-tier and replacement-level players."**
   (The framing claim.)

**Data sources:**
- Cannon/Fisher/Fellingham/Page, "Analyzing the effects of NBA head
  coaches," JQAS 2025 (DOI 10.1515/jqas-2025-0025). Method: probit
  monotone BART on ΔtVORP.
- Basketball-Reference VORP, team data, coach tenure.
- `nba_api.LeagueDashLineups` for possession-weighted lineup data
  (needed for ΔtVORP).
- Pro Sports Transactions for injury data.
- `bartpy` (Python) or `BART` (R) for the BART implementation.

**Methodology sketch:**
- Build ΔtVORP: per-possession difference between offensive and
  defensive aggregate VORP for the 10 men on the floor.
- Fit a probit-link monotone BART model: win-probability as a function
  of ΔtVORP + coach fixed effects.
- Extract coach posterior distributions; rank-order, highlight where
  uncertainty is wide (Nurse small sample) vs. narrow (Pop, Phil,
  Riley long tenure).
- Show 3 "the roster was hiding him" cases (Vogel, etc.) and 3 "the
  roster was carrying him" cases.

**Risks:**
- Is ΔtVORP itself coach-contaminated? (Partly yes — VORP captures the
  on-court product the coach influenced. Address head-on as a
  limitation, motivate the BART regularization.)
- Endogeneity of coach hiring (good orgs hire good coaches). Be
  explicit.
- Small-sample tail (Nurse 2 seasons). Show posterior width on screen.

**Prior work:** Cannon et al. JQAS 2025 (the source paper, never
visualized). Earlier fixed-effects analysis on TowardsDataScience
(808 team-seasons, 1979–2019) found similar magnitudes. **Zero video
treatment of either.**

**Effort:** ~3 weeks. The paper does most of the methodology work for
you — your contribution is the animation, the named-coach reveals, and
the responsible uncertainty visualization.

---

# IDEA 6 — The NBA Pulled Its Tracking Data. Here's What's Still On GitHub.

> "In 2017 the NBA stopped publicly releasing player tracking data.
> 600 games of 25 Hz coordinates from 2015–16 are still sitting on
> GitHub and HuggingFace. Curry ran 2.4 miles per game off-ball that
> season. The league average was 1.6. Here's what else the data still
> says."

**Channel fit:** Investigative framing, casual-fan accessible, visual
gold (top-down player trajectories in Manim), but methodology-defensible.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant:** "Drop the conspiracy framing. Reframe as 'the last public
  tracking data, and what we learned.' Whitehead, Narsu, dcayton himself
  have all published on this corpus."
- **Bettor:** "10-year-old data doesn't price 2026 markets. Watch but
  don't bookmark."
- **Casual (TOP 1):** "Conspiracy + Curry + a number I want to know.
  Instant click."
- **FO insider:** "Frame the question as: what generalizes forward
  despite rule changes? Hand-checking unchanged since, but defensive
  3-second enforcement has shifted."
- **Strategist (honorable):** "Investigative angle is rare in this
  niche and Jxmy-coded. Open with one shocking 2026-relevant claim
  before going historical."

**Headline candidates:**
1. **"Curry ran 2.4 miles per game off-ball in 2015–16. League average
   was 1.6. No public dataset since has been able to verify whether that
   changed."** (Single hard number, specific player.)
2. **"Of the 600 games still public, LeBron only doubled the pick-and-
   roll [X]% of the time. The league average was [Y]%. Here's how he
   defended it instead."** (Methodology demo on a known player.)
3. **"The five questions only the 2015–16 data can still answer."**
   (Listicle frame, multiple visual segments.)

**Data sources:**
- `neilmj/BasketballData` (canonical raw mirror).
- HuggingFace `dcayton/nba_tracking_data_15_16` (parsed + PBP-merged).
- `linouk23/NBA-Player-Movements` (visualization tooling).
- `sealneaward/nba-movement-data` (cleaning tooling).
- `keisuke198619/SportVU_extraction` (extraction tooling).

**Methodology sketch:**
- Pick 5–7 questions answerable only with tracking data:
  off-ball mileage per player, defensive coverage choice on PnR,
  defender displacement caused by off-ball stars, average shot-defender
  distance per shooter, screen frequency by team.
- Each question gets a Manim segment with actual possession overlays.
- Compare each finding to whatever public 2025-26 derivative exists
  (NBA stats tracking page) to honestly bound "what has changed."

**Risks:**
- Data is 10 years old. Frame as "what we can still learn" not "current
  insights."
- The "league doesn't want us to see" framing is conspiracy bait. Stay
  honest: it was a vendor change (Second Spectrum in 2017, Sony
  Hawk-Eye in 2023), not a coverup.

**Prior work:** Todd Whitehead, Krishna Narsu, Andrew Patton, dcayton
himself — all have published on this corpus. **Differentiate via
visual treatment + the "last public dataset" investigative frame.**

**Effort:** ~6 weeks. Data wrangling is real; visualizations are
the bulk.

---

# IDEA 7 — The Two Load-Management Studies That Can't Both Be Right

> "In 2024 the NBA published a report saying load management doesn't
> reduce injury risk. Last month a peer-reviewed paper using similar
> data found a 75% reduction. Here's exactly where they disagree —
> and which one survives the methodology."

**Channel fit:** Adversarial methodology piece, Jxmy register,
controversial topic everyone has takes on. Punch-up potential.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant (top 3):** "Highest-leverage punch-up idea on the doc.
  Recreation work, not heavy modeling."
- **Bettor:** "If you can predict which stars actually play in B2Bs,
  that's prop and DFS gold. The dataset you'd build to answer the
  methodology question is a player-availability model."
- **Casual:** "Two studies, opposite answers' is a great hook. Just
  pick a side — 'here's who's right' is way more clickable than 'both
  are noisy.'"
- **FO insider:** "Teams settled this internally — load mgmt reduces
  soft-tissue injury, doesn't affect contact injury. Split by injury
  type."
- **Strategist (honorable):** "Best chance at a non-quant breakout on
  the doc. 'Lying' overpromises; 'disagrees' is safer and still clicks."

**Headline candidates:**
1. **"Three out of four times you can flip the NBA's load-management
   conclusion just by changing how you define 'rest.'"** (Methodology-
   forward.)
2. **"Load management reduces soft-tissue injury by X%. It doesn't
   affect contact injury at all. The aggregated study averages those
   into nothing."** (The FO split — best for "I'm right and here's
   why both papers are wrong.")
3. **"The NBA's 2024 report and the 2026 arXiv paper used the same
   players, the same season, and got opposite answers. Here's the
   four words that flipped the result."** (Specific reframe.)

**Data sources:**
- NBA's 2024 official report (ESPN coverage at espn.com/.../39288379).
- arXiv 2603.26935 (the 2026 reanalysis — verify it survived review
  before citing on screen).
- Rotowire injury history (journalist-curated, not clinical).
- Jeff Stotts / In Street Clothes for clinical-grade injury classification.
- nba_api game logs for minutes / rest patterns.

**Methodology sketch:**
- Recreate both analyses on a common dataset.
- Identify exact divergence: definition of "load," lag window, control
  group, injury classification.
- Run both methodologies side-by-side. Show where they overlap and
  where they diverge.
- Split outcome by injury type (soft-tissue vs. contact vs. load-related).
  Predict that load management matters for soft-tissue only.

**Risks:**
- Need to confirm the arXiv paper exists and isn't a preprint that
  won't survive review.
- "75% reduction" smells like a base-rate trick (rare event + small
  denominator). Bound it carefully.
- Injury definitions are the methodology landmine.

**Prior work:** Jeff Stotts (In Street Clothes); Keith Smith on minutes/
load; the Lewis et al. AFL load papers the NBA work derives from.

**Effort:** ~3 weeks. Bulk is recreation work.

---

# IDEA 8 — Whose Defense Is Real? The Matchup-Data Audit

> "There's an `nba_api` endpoint that gives you possession-level
> defender assignments — who actually guarded whom for how many seconds.
> Almost nobody uses it. I matched defensive reputations against actual
> matchup load and found that one of the league's vaunted stoppers
> guards average offensive players 70% of the time."

**Channel fit:** Quant-leaning but accessible, novel data source,
named-player findings, pairs with #5 (Coach Effect) as the "the
things the public underrates" series.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant:** Replacement candidate. Novel API use. Defensive
  reputations are notoriously narrative-driven. This is the kind of
  audit the APBRmetrics audience loves.
- **Bettor:** "DPOY futures market and player-prop coverage matter
  here. If you can identify who actually guards stars, that's a
  matchup-prop edge."
- **Casual:** "Lukewarm — defense feels less exciting than offense.
  Save it with named-player reveals."
- **FO insider:** "The matchup endpoint is good but it doesn't
  capture off-ball assignments or help rotations. Frame the limitation."
- **Strategist:** "Defense videos struggle for retention. Lead with
  the strongest 'X is overrated' reveal; bury the methodology."

**Headline candidates:**
1. **"Jrue Holiday's defensive reputation is 90% real. OG Anunoby's is
   60% real. [X]'s is a myth."** (Three specific named-player calls.)
2. **"The five wings the public thinks guard stars but actually guard
   bench players."**
3. **"NBA defensive reputation correlates 0.4 with actual matchup
   difficulty. The public is fooled by switches that never happen."**

**Data sources:**
- `nba_api.BoxScoreMatchupsV3` — possession-by-possession defender
  assignments. **Almost unused for public video work.**
- Defensive reputation: All-Defense voting, DPOY votes, public
  perception polls.
- Offensive load metric: opponent's offensive RAPM or EPM.

**Methodology sketch:**
- For every "elite defender" candidate (top-30 DRtg, All-Defense,
  DPOY vote-getter), pull season-long matchup data.
- Compute weighted offensive-load-faced = Σ(possessions × opponent EPM).
- Rank defenders by load-faced, compare to defensive reputation
  rankings.
- Identify the "fakes" (high reputation, low load) and the "hidden"
  (low reputation, high load).
- Manim treatment: matchup possession breakdowns for 3 named players.

**Risks:**
- The matchup endpoint only captures primary on-ball assignments. Off-ball
  help, schemes, and rotations aren't directly visible. Frame as
  partial picture.
- Some defenders are hidden by scheme choice (drop-coverage rim
  protectors don't get matchup credit for the wing they helped on).

**Prior work:** Krishna Narsu (BBall Index has a matchup-difficulty
metric, paywalled); Todd Whitehead. **Public video treatment of the
matchup data does not exist.** This is novel API use.

**Effort:** ~3 weeks. Endpoint is structured; methodology is
straightforward; main work is the named-player reveal sequencing.

---

# IDEA 9 — Wasted Gravity

> "The NBA released its official 'gravity' stat last season. Curry leads
> by 50%. But the leaderboard misses the entire half that matters:
> gravity that doesn't translate to scoring is a coaching failure, not
> a player skill. Here's whose gravity is wasted."

**Channel fit:** Pure visual-explainer territory. Vector field viz,
Curry footage, NBA's official stat as the news peg. Casual-friendly.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant:** "Compare your viz to BBall Index's existing Gravity score
  head-to-head. Density-field viz with arbitrary KDE bandwidth is
  visually compelling and statistically arbitrary — be honest about it."
- **Bettor:** "Pretty pictures. No bet."
- **Casual (top 3):** "Curry thumbnail = built-in CTR floor. More
  Curry footage, less leaderboard talk."
- **FO insider:** "The leaderboard is fan framing. Wasted gravity —
  gravity that doesn't translate to scoring — is the FO question, and
  it's a coaching/scheme indictment, not a player one. Lean there."
- **Strategist:** "Calibrated. NBA-stat news peg is fresh. Density-field
  methodology caveat should be a 20-second on-screen disclaimer max."

**Headline candidates:**
1. **"Curry generates 4.2 feet of average defender displacement
   off-ball. Half of it gets wasted. The Warriors' scheme converts only
   X% of his gravity into a shot attempt."** (Player-level + scheme
   indictment.)
2. **"NBA Gravity leaderboard top 5: Curry, [X], [Y], [Z], [W].
   Wasted-Gravity leaderboard: completely different. Which one is
   actually valuable?"**
3. **"Two players, identical gravity scores. One unlocks 12 points per
   game for his team. The other unlocks 3."** (Cleanest comparison.)

**Data sources:**
- 2015–16 SportVU corpus for player position density fields
  (`dcayton/nba_tracking_data_15_16`).
- NBA's official 2025–26 Gravity stat (nba.com/news/intro-to-gravity-
  stat-nba-2025).
- nba_api shot tracking for "did the gravity convert" measurement.
- BBall Index's existing Gravity metric (Krishna Narsu) for cross-
  validation.

**Methodology sketch:**
- For each possession, compute defender-position density field around
  each offensive player.
- Derive vector field of defender displacement caused by each player.
- Match displacement events to subsequent shot outcomes (same
  possession): did the gravity produce a shot? Did the shot go in?
- Compute Wasted Gravity = gravity events that didn't produce a
  team-level shot attempt within 3 seconds.
- Manim: top-down half-court, Curry off-ball, defenders bending
  toward him in heat-map orange, then either flowing into a shot
  trajectory or dissipating.

**Risks:**
- Density-field methodology has arbitrary KDE choices. Cap the claims.
- The 2015–16 data is a different basketball than 2025–26. Caveat for
  era effects.
- Schemes change; coaches change; what looks like wasted gravity could
  be downstream value not captured by shot attempts.

**Prior work:** Seth Partnow's older "gravity score" pre-NBA-official;
Krishna Narsu's BBall Index Gravity; Todd Whitehead's defender-distance
work; Kirk Goldsberry on gravity (Grantland archives). **No public
video treatment of gravity as a vector field.**

**Effort:** ~5 weeks. Vector-field rendering is non-trivial in Manim.

---

# IDEA 10 — The Ring-Chase Tax (Reimbursement-Adjusted)

> "Every July a veteran takes a 'discount' to chase a ring. After you
> adjust for the league's vet-minimum reimbursement, that discount is
> smaller than fans think. Real cost to the contender per ring-chase
> signing: [X]. Real championship-probability bump bought: [Y]%."

**Channel fit:** Builds on the contracts pipeline. Clean hook. Narrow
audience but high Discord-shareability.

**Persona heatmap:**
- **Quant:** "Yes, if the '$ per ring %' exchange rate is computed
  with a proper bootstrap, not point estimates. 'Market value' for 30+
  vets is exactly where your model is weakest."
- **Bettor:** "The real edge is the minute-allocation prediction on
  ring-chase signings vs. their O/U player props. Fade the unders on
  guys on min deals who'll get role-player minutes."
- **Casual:** "Name names. CP3, Iguodala, PJ Tucker. Make me feel
  something."
- **FO insider:** "The vet min is reimbursed by the league above year
  2 of service — so the team-cap-cost is artificially compressed.
  Most public surplus models miss this. Big reframe."
- **Strategist (honorable):** "Title is sharp. Risk that the headline
  is more interesting than the variance supports."

**Headline candidates:**
1. **"After league reimbursement, the typical ring-chase signing costs
   the contender only $X — not the $Y everyone quotes. Which makes the
   contender side of this deal much better than the player side."**
2. **"Veterans pay $Z million per 1% increase in championship
   probability. The median ring-chase pays for X% — and 80% of them
   don't get the ring."**
3. **"Six historic ring-chases. Three got the ring. Three didn't. Here's
   what they all left on the table — and what they took home."**

**Data sources:**
- Spotrac contract history (already in hand).
- Vegas preseason title odds at signing date (sportsbookreviewsonline.com
  archives).
- League vet-min reimbursement schedule (CBA Article II details).
- Final outcomes (championship results).

**Methodology sketch:**
- Define a ring-chaser: vet ≥ 30, signed for ≤ 80% of expected market
  value, contender (preseason title odds ≥ 15%).
- Compute nominal discount (market value − actual signing).
- Compute *team-cost* discount: subtract the vet-min reimbursement
  (full reimbursement for vets ≥ 2 years' service on minimum contracts).
- Compute $/ring% exchange rate via bootstrap on the realized-outcome
  cohort.
- Anchor on 5–6 named cases.

**Risks:**
- Player-option-year-2 optionality has real value the model needs to
  price.
- 10-day and hardship contracts complicate the dataset; exclude with
  clear filter.
- Endorsement income flowing from playoff visibility isn't captured.
  Acknowledge.

**Prior work:** Owen Phillips on minimum-contract veterans; Seth Partnow
in *The Midrange Theory* on diminishing returns; Ben Falk at CTG. **Nobody
publishes the vet-min-reimbursement-adjusted discount or the dollar/ring-%
exchange rate.** This is the FO-insider reframe.

**Effort:** ~3 weeks.

---

# Appendix A — What Got Cut and Why

**Dropped from v0:**

- **"The Three GMs Who Built Half the League's Surplus"** — Quant
  pointed out it's dominated by drafting luck (Presti = Shai at #11,
  Pelinka = Reaves UDFA), not negotiation skill. The FO insider added
  that GM-attribution is fan/media framing — inside teams, contracts
  are the output of a cap strategist, a pro scout director, an agent
  relationship, a coach. Could be salvaged as **"Which front offices
  extract the most surplus per CBA tool?"** but the channel
  decomposition is a different (smaller) video, and the headline number
  wouldn't carry it. Cut.

- **"When DARKO, EPM, and LEBRON Disagree"** — Quant flagged it as a
  Twitter-thread topic without an interview-driven angle. LEBRON is
  paywalled at BBall Index. The actual fresh version requires
  conversations with Snarr or Medvedovsky on camera, which is outside
  the solo-creator scope. The casual fan agreed it's
  inside-baseball without a polarizing-player hook. Cut.

**Considered, parked for later:**

- **Referee bias revisited.** The 2007 Price-Wolfers paper found
  same-race foul bias; the 2023 *Scientific Reports* paper found it has
  vanished post-publication. "The paper that made the NBA fix itself"
  is a legitimate framing but the deep research didn't land a hard
  headline number. B+ candidate. Park.

- **Curry's shot-selection contagion.** Quantifiable (27-foot makes
  747 → 3,658 since 2016; off-the-dribble 3PA 23 → 28%). But well-trod
  in print (WBUR, NBA.com, Dartmouth analytics blog). Would need a
  counterfactual ("what does the league look like if Curry's 2016
  doesn't happen?") to differentiate. B candidate. Park.

- **The Trade Deadline Edge.** Pelton's grades are paywalled prose, not
  structured data. The "value-tracked-over-time" dataset doesn't
  exist publicly. Would be a 3-month side project to build the dataset
  alone. Skip.

# Appendix B — Suggested Production Sequencing

If you're prioritizing strategic channel growth:

1. **Idea 3 (Tanking → Trade Chips)** first. Strategist top pick;
   casual top pick; topical (post-2026 Finals, post-OKC championship);
   evergreen interest; tight 4-week effort. Sets up the channel
   reputation for "elegant arithmetic on a topic everyone has takes
   on."

2. **Idea 2 (Two Stars, Two Halves)** second. Bettor top pick, FO top
   pick, direct continuation of the contracts methodology. Builds the
   audience that converted from the "1 of 97" video.

3. **Idea 1 (Apron Game Tree)** third. Unanimous strongest. By video
   #3 you have the visual reputation to land a 6-week simulation piece.

4. **Idea 5 (Coach Effect)** fourth. Replicable in 3 weeks; new
   audience; specific named-coach reveals are highly shareable; pairs
   thematically with #2 as "underrated NBA stuff."

5. **Idea 4 (EPV From Scratch)** fifth or held as the channel-anniversary
   flagship. 8–10 weeks of work; must have established audience first;
   compounds the channel for 18+ months after publishing.

If you're prioritizing what you *want* to make:
- The flagship (4) is the soul of the channel.
- The apron sim (1) is the highest-quality methodology piece.
- The tanking reframe (3) is the cleanest argument.

Choose accordingly. All ten are defensible. Make the ones you'd be
proud to defend in the Discord at 2am.
